claims.excerpt: Market projections point to 500-600MW by 2030, roughly three times current capacity.

claims.excerpt: a 500MW development in central Poland, a 950MW project in Western Pomerania (150km from Berlin), and a 3.2GW opportunity on Poland's north coast

claims.claim: ResearchAndMarkets states Poland's installed IT load capacity is forecast to rise from 660 MW in 2025 to 930 MW in 2030, a 7.34% CAGR.
claims.sourceTitle: Poland Data Center - Market Share Analysis, Industry Trends & Statistics, Growth Forecasts (2026-2031)
claims.excerpt: the nation's installed IT load capacity is forecast to rise from 660 MW in 2025 to 930 MW in 2030, a 7.34% CAGR that signals a pivot toward AI-optimized, high-density deployments

tensions.text: PMR/Atman's claim that Poland's ~200 MW (2024) commercial DC capacity is 'more than one-third of the total capacity across the entire CEE region' implies a CEE-wide capacity of roughly 600 MW — starkly at odds with the geo.PL derivation's denominator of Statista's CE-7 colo capacity of 3,451 MW (2025). A ~6x gap between these CEE-region totals undermines confidence in the 0.07 ratio's denominator.

datacenterHawk's 'current' Poland capacity figure of 220 MW (2026) closely matches Statista's 228 MW colo estimate (2025) used in the geo.PL derivation, but DC Byte reports 481 MW (2024) and 605 MW total supply for a seemingly similar colocation-inclusive scope — a ~2-3x discrepancy among sources purporting to measure comparable current capacity, which the derivation does not address.

Mordor's own 660 MW (2025) installed-load figure, cited in the geo.PL cross-check as producing a ~0.10 ratio, is corroborated by ResearchAndMarkets (660 MW→930 MW, 7.34% CAGR) and Prime East (660 MW→930-1000 MW, ~7% CAGR), suggesting the 'installed load' scope is well-supported by multiple independent sources — arguably deserving more weight than the single Statista colo figure anchoring the 0.07 point estimate.

absences: No source provides a country-by-country CE-7/CEE colocation capacity breakdown to independently verify Statista's 3,451 MW regional denominator used in the geo.PL derivation.
No single consistent-scope (e.g., PMR commercial-DC) capacity series was found covering multiple CE countries, despite the node's stated promotion path requiring exactly this.

No reconciliation across sources of the wide range of 'current' Poland capacity figures (200 MW, 220 MW, 228 MW, 481 MW, 605 MW, 660 MW) by segment (colocation vs. installed IT load vs. commercial DC vs. total supply).

No quantified grid-capacity or power-availability constraint figures despite repeated qualitative references to 'grid capacity' and a 'gap between data center demand and available grid capacity' as the defining risk variable.
No data on actual utilization/occupancy rates for Polish colocation supply, only aggregate capacity and demand narratives.
